mnemonics.title: Cloud Pricing Models
mnemonics.content: PRIS: Pay-as-you-go, Reserved, Instance, Spot - discount depth increases with commitment level

mnemonics.title: TCO Components
mnemonics.content: HARDWARE + SOFTWARE + FACILITIES + PERSONNEL + MAINTENANCE + OPERATIONS = Total Cost of Ownership

mnemonics.title: Cost Optimization Steps
mnemonics.content: RIGHT: Review usage, Identify waste, Generate savings plan, Handle optimization, Track results

mnemonics.title: Scaling Types
mnemonics.content: V-H-V: Vertical adds power to existing, Horizontal adds more units, Vs are limited by hardware

acronyms.usage: Upfront investment in physical infrastructure, common in on-premise setup
Pay-as-you-go model, paying only for consumed cloud resources
Complete cost analysis including hardware, software, personnel, maintenance over lifetime
Measure of profitability from cloud migration: (Net Benefits / Cost) x 100
Hourly/per-second billing with no commitment required
Long-term commitment pricing with up to 60-70% discount
Major cloud provider by Amazon offering compute, storage, and services
Managing infrastructure through code for cost-effective and automated deployment

visualTips.visualization: Think of CapEx as buying a house (large upfront, own asset) and OpEx as renting an apartment (pay monthly for what you use)
Vertical = growing UP (adding power to single tower), Horizontal = growing OUT (building more towers)
Reserved = season ticket (discount, commitment), Spot = last-minute ticket (cheapest, may be cancelled), On-demand = walk-in (full price, flexible)
Think of a restaurant that automatically opens/closes tables based on customer count - open more when busy, close when quiet to save costs

analogies.analogy: Like a utility bill - you only pay for electricity/water you consume, not for power plant infrastructure
Like airline standby tickets - available at huge discounts but you might not get on the flight if demand is high
Like a taxi meter - you pay only when the car is moving (code executing), not when parked (idle)

analogies.analization: Like wearing properly fitted clothes - too big wastes fabric (over-provisioned wastes money), too small doesn't work (under-provisioned causes issues)
Like having multiple credit cards from different banks - each offers deals, you can switch to get best rates

analogies.analogy: Like buying in bulk at wholesale clubs - the more you (and millions of others) buy, the cheaper it becomes per unit
